content.full_text:
California 
Fair Political 
Practices Commission 
Bruce Risley 
Civic Action League of 
Morro Bay Inc. 
P.O. Box 245 
Morro Bay, CA 
93442 
Dear Mr. Risley: 
September 27, 1989 
Re: 
Our File No. G-89-557 
We have received your letter, dated September 17, 1989, 
concerning a possible violation of the conflict of interest 
provisions of the Political Reform Actl by a member of the Morro 
Bay City council. 
Enclosed are a pamphlet explaining the conflict 
of interest laws and a complaint form. 
The Commission does not provide advice concerning past 
conduct of public officials; furthermore, we only provide advice 
to public officials and their authorized representatives. 
Complaints about possible violations of the Political Reform Act 
are handled by our Enforcement Division. 
After you review the 
enclosed pamphlet, you may wish to file a complaint with the 
Enforcement Division concerning possible conflict of interest 
violations. 
If you have any questions about filing a complaint, 
please contact the Enforcement Division at (916) 322-6441. 
Sincerely, 
~~1~ 
~ .J)-t-l~%U~~ 
, 
I 
Kathryn E. Donovan 
General Counsel 
Government Code Sections 81000-91015. 
428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916)322-5660 


CIVIC ACTION LEAGUE OFMORROBAY INC. 
PO BOX 245 
MORRO BAY CA. 93442 
Fair political Practices Commission 
428 J Street 
Sacramento, CA 95814 
September 17, 1989

or should have been allmved to vote on alternative solutions to finding nevi

According to his forms 721 Statements of Economic Interest, on file for 
the four years 1981 through 1984 as well as 1987, the Council member vias 
employee by the California Precast Concrete Pipe Association as Managing 
Engineer. 
(Presumably he 'iv-as also so employed in 1985 and 1986 but he 'ivas 
out of public office then.) 
Each year that he filed a report on this he 
listed his income from this trade association in the over $10,000 category 
except for 1987 when he listed it as betvleen $1,000 and $10,000. 
His home 
address was listed w'i th the Secretary of State as the address of this 
manufacturer's organization. 
While working for this organization, whose purpose is to promote use 
of precast pipe, he was voting on the City Council (and still does) on 
matters pertaining to finding a nevi source of vlater for our city. Recently, 
he has been active in trying to eliminate the job of the City viator Engineer 
who ivas specifically hired to find new" sources of vlater for our city. 
Local 
solutions pursued by the Water Engineer are: 
1. Drilling for ne,"; vlells in local water basins 
2. 
Developing a stream divers ion program vlhereby local streams Ilould 
be diverted into reservoirs during rainy seasons and thus be 
tapped in dry periocs. 
3. 
Relocating wells 
4. 
Replacing leaJ<:ing pipes 
This particular Council member has been a strong acvocate of the State 
Water Project. 
According to Clint Milne, San Luis Obispo Deputy County 
Engineer, the California State Water Project will, if completed, use 142 miles 
of precast pipe and will cost the State, exclusive of installation, $230 million 
dollars. 
In summary our question is, did and does this Council meml::er's employment 
for the California Precast Pipe Association constitute a conflict of interest 
when voting on water shortage solution choices? 
Than].::: you for your attention in this matter.

sections.question: We request advice as to whether a member of the City Council should be or should have been allowed to vote on alternative solutions to finding new water supplies for our city under the following circumstances: According to his forms 721 Statements of Economic Interest, on file for the four years 1981 through 1984 as well as 1987, the Council member was employee by the California Precast Concrete Pipe Association as Managing Engineer. (Presumably he was also so employed in 1985 and 1986 but he was out of public office then.) Each year that he filed a report on this he listed his income from this trade association in the over $10,000 category except for 1987 when he listed it as between $1,000 and $10,000. His home address was listed with the Secretary of State as the address of this manufacturer's organization. While working for this organization, whose purpose is to promote use of precast pipe, he was voting on the City Council (and still does) on matters pertaining to finding a new source of water for our city. Recently, he has been active in trying to eliminate the job of the City water Engineer who was specifically hired to find new sources of water for our city. Local solutions pursued by the Water Engineer are: 1. Drilling for new wells in local water basins 2. Developing a stream diversion program whereby local streams would be diverted into reservoirs during rainy seasons and thus be tapped in dry periods. 3. Relocating wells 4. Replacing leaking pipes. This particular Council member has been a strong advocate of the State Water Project. According to Clint Milne, San Luis Obispo Deputy County Engineer, the California State Water Project will, if completed, use 142 miles of precast pipe and will cost the State, exclusive of installation, $230 million dollars. In summary our question is, did and does this Council member's employment for the California Precast Pipe Association constitute a conflict of interest when voting on water shortage solution choices?
sections.conclusion: The Commission does not provide advice concerning past conduct of public officials; furthermore, we only provide advice to public officials and their authorized representatives. Complaints about possible violations of the Political Reform Act are handled by our Enforcement Division. After you review the enclosed pamphlet, you may wish to file a complaint with the Enforcement Division concerning possible conflict of interest violations.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether a City Council member's employment with the California Precast Concrete Pipe Association constitutes a conflict of interest when voting on city water supply solutions, particularly given the State Water Project's proposed use of 142 miles of precast pipe.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC declined to provide advice on this matter because it only advises public officials and their authorized representatives, not members of the public. The complainant is directed to file a formal complaint with the Enforcement Division if they believe a conflict of interest violation occurred.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document includes both the incoming request (dated September 17, 1989) and the FPPC response letter (dated September 27, 1989). Response letter declines to provide advice and redirects to Enforcement Division. No formal CONCLUSION section header, but conclusion text clearly stated in body of response.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: We request advice as to whether a member of the City Council should be or should have been allowed to vote on alternative solutions to finding new water supplies for our city under the following circumstances: According to his forms 721 Statements of Economic Interest, on file for the four years 1981 through 1984 as well as 1987, the Council member was employee by the California Precast Concrete Pipe Association as Managing Engineer. (Presumably he was also so employed in 1985 and 1986 but he was out of public office then.) Each year that he filed a report on this he listed his income from this trade association in the over $10,000 category except for 1987 when he listed it as between $1,000 and $10,000. His home address was listed with the Secretary of State as the address of this manufacturer's organization. While working for this organization, whose purpose is to promote use of precast pipe, he was voting on the City Council (and still does) on matters pertaining to finding a new source of water for our city. Recently, he has been active in trying to eliminate the job of the City water Engineer who was specifically hired to find new sources of water for our city. Local solutions pursued by the Water Engineer are: 1. Drilling for new wells in local water basins 2. Developing a stream diversion program whereby local streams would be diverted into reservoirs during rainy seasons and thus be tapped in dry periods. 3. Relocating wells 4. Replacing leaking pipes. This particular Council member has been a strong advocate of the State Water Project. According to Clint Milne, San Luis Obispo Deputy County Engineer, the California State Water Project will, if completed, use 142 miles of precast pipe and will cost the State, exclusive of installation, $230 million dollars. In summary our question is, did and does this Council member's employment for the California Precast Pipe Association constitute a conflict of interest when voting on water shortage solution choices?

CONCLUSION: The Commission does not provide advice concerning past conduct of public officials; furthermore, we only provide advice to public officials and their authorized representatives. Complaints about possible violations of the Political Reform Act are handled by our Enforcement Division. After you review the enclosed pamphlet, you may wish to file a complaint with the Enforcement Division concerning possible conflict of interest violations.

embedding.summary: The FPPC received a complaint from a citizen organization regarding a potential conflict of interest involving a Morro Bay City Council member's concurrent employment with the California Precast Concrete Pipe Association and voting on city water supply solutions. The FPPC declined to provide advice, directing the complainant to file a formal complaint with the Enforcement Division.
